a: Oracles of judgment and hope for Judah and Jerusalem in the days of Uzziah through Hezekiah. Isaiah indicts a rebellious nation (1) yet foresees the mountain of the Lord drawing the nations (2); the song of the vineyard exposes Judah's fruitlessness (5); Isaiah is commissioned at his temple vision of the thrice-holy Lord (6); the Immanuel sign is given to Ahaz amid the Syro-Ephraimite crisis (7); and a child is promised who is Wonderful Counselor, Mighty God, on whom the government rests (9). The shoot from the stump of Jesse ushers in a peaceable kingdom (11), closing with a song of thanksgiving (12).
summary: Judgment and hope for Judah, with Isaiah's temple call (6), the Immanuel sign (7), the child-king (9), and the shoot of Jesse (11).

a: Oracles against the nations followed by an apocalyptic finale. Burdens fall on Babylon (13–14, with the taunt over the fallen morning star), Moab, Damascus, Cush, Egypt, and Tyre, showing the Lord's sovereignty over every power. The section climaxes in the so-called Isaiah Apocalypse (24–27): the earth is laid waste under universal judgment, yet the Lord prepares a feast on the mountain and swallows up death forever (25), the dead are raised (26), and Israel is gathered like a fruitful vineyard restored (27).
summary: Oracles against the nations (Babylon, Moab, Egypt, Tyre) capped by the Isaiah Apocalypse where the Lord swallows up death (25).

a: A series of woes against Judah's false security alternating with promises of deliverance. Ephraim's drunkards and Jerusalem's leaders are rebuked, yet the Lord lays in Zion a tested cornerstone (28); woe falls on those who seek alliance with Egypt rather than trusting the Holy One (30–31). A righteous king and a transformed land are promised (32), and the section ends with the ransomed of the Lord returning on the Way of Holiness to Zion with everlasting joy (35).
summary: Woes on Judah's false security with the tested cornerstone in Zion (28) and the Way of Holiness for the ransomed (35).

a: The historical narrative bridge centered on Hezekiah. Sennacherib's Assyrian army besieges Jerusalem and the Rabshakeh taunts the city (36); Hezekiah prays, Isaiah promises deliverance, and the angel of the Lord strikes the Assyrian camp (37). Hezekiah falls mortally ill, prays, and is granted fifteen more years with the sign of the shadow's retreat (38); but he shows his treasures to Babylonian envoys, prompting Isaiah's prophecy of future exile to Babylon (39).
summary: Hezekiah's reign: Jerusalem delivered from Sennacherib (36–37), the king's healing (38), and the Babylonian envoys foretelling exile (39).

a: The Book of Comfort, addressing exiles with the promise of return and redemption. It opens "Comfort, comfort my people" and the voice crying in the wilderness (40); the Lord proves himself the only God against idols and names Cyrus as his anointed deliverer (44–45). At its heart stand the Servant Songs, climaxing in the suffering servant who is pierced for our transgressions and bears the sin of many (52:13–53:12). It closes with a free invitation to the thirsty to come and the certainty that God's word accomplishes its purpose (55).
summary: The Book of Comfort: God versus idols, Cyrus the deliverer, the Servant Songs and the suffering servant (53), and the gospel invitation (55).

a: The closing vision of a restored and inclusive Zion alongside warnings against hypocrisy. Foreigners and eunuchs are welcomed to God's house of prayer for all peoples (56); true fasting is justice and mercy (58); sin separates the people from God until the Redeemer comes to Zion (59). The Spirit-anointed herald proclaims good news to the poor and the year of the Lord's favor (61), and the book ends with the promise of new heavens and a new earth (65–66).
summary: A restored, inclusive Zion: God's house of prayer for all peoples (56), true fasting (58), the anointed herald (61), and new heavens and earth (65–66).

a: The call and commissioning of Jeremiah in the days of Josiah. The Lord declares he knew and consecrated Jeremiah as a prophet to the nations before he was formed in the womb; Jeremiah objects that he is only a youth, but God touches his mouth and puts his words in it. Two signs follow: the almond branch, showing God watching over his word, and the boiling pot tilting from the north, foretelling the disaster Babylon will bring. Jeremiah is appointed to pluck up and tear down, to build and to plant.
summary: Jeremiah's call as prophet to the nations, with the almond branch and the boiling pot from the north.

a: The great body of oracles indicting Judah for covenant unfaithfulness. Judah has forsaken the fountain of living waters for broken cisterns (2) and committed spiritual adultery; Jeremiah delivers the temple sermon warning that the house bearing God's name will fall like Shiloh (7). The prophet voices his "confessions" of anguish and complaint, and enacts signs such as the ruined linen loincloth and the potter at the wheel (13, 18–19). He foretells seventy years of Babylonian exile and the cup of God's wrath for the nations (25).
summary: Oracles against Judah's unfaithfulness: broken cisterns (2), the temple sermon (7), the potter (18), and seventy years of exile (25).

a: Conflict between Jeremiah and false prophets over Judah's fate. Jeremiah is put on trial for the temple sermon and narrowly escapes death (26); he wears a yoke to symbolize submission to Babylon, while Hananiah breaks a yoke and falsely predicts swift deliverance, then dies as Jeremiah foretold (27–28). Jeremiah writes a letter to the exiles already in Babylon, urging them to build houses, plant gardens, and seek the welfare of the city, for the exile will last seventy years before God's plans for hope are fulfilled (29).
summary: Jeremiah versus the false prophets: his temple trial (26), the yoke and Hananiah (27–28), and the letter to the exiles (29).

a: The Book of Consolation, a cluster of restoration promises amid the coming judgment. God promises to restore the fortunes of Israel and Judah and to heal their wounds (30); he announces a new covenant in which the law is written on the heart and sins are remembered no more (31). From prison Jeremiah buys a field at Anathoth as a sign that houses and fields will again be bought in the land (32), and God reaffirms the covenant with David and a righteous Branch (33).
summary: The Book of Consolation: restoration, the new covenant (31), the field at Anathoth (32), and the righteous Branch of David (33).

a: Narratives of Jerusalem's final days and its aftermath. The faithful Rechabites are commended (35); Jehoiakim burns Jeremiah's scroll, which is rewritten (36); Jeremiah is imprisoned and lowered into a muddy cistern for his Babylon message (37–38). Jerusalem falls and the temple is destroyed (39); Gedaliah is appointed governor but assassinated, and the remnant flees to Egypt against God's word, dragging Jeremiah with them (40–43). Jeremiah pronounces judgment on the idolatry of the Egyptian community and gives a personal word of preservation to Baruch (45).
summary: Jerusalem's fall and aftermath: the burned scroll (36), the muddy cistern (38), the city's destruction (39), and flight to Egypt (43–44).

a: Oracles against the nations followed by a historical appendix. Judgment is pronounced on Egypt, the Philistines, Moab, Ammon, Edom, Damascus, Kedar, Elam, and at greatest length Babylon, whose fall is foretold even as it is God's instrument (46–51). The book closes with a historical appendix recounting the fall of Jerusalem, the temple's destruction and the carrying off of its furnishings, the exile, and the release of King Jehoiachin from prison in Babylon (52).
summary: Oracles against the nations, climaxing in Babylon's fall (50–51), with a closing account of Jerusalem's destruction and Jehoiachin's release (52).

a: Two acrostic dirges over the fall of Jerusalem. The first poem personifies the city as a widow weeping bitterly in the night, deserted by lovers and friends, mourning that no one comforts her as she confesses the Lord is righteous and she has rebelled (1). The second poem describes the Lord himself as an enemy who has swallowed up Zion in his fierce anger, casting down her ramparts and rejecting his altar, while children faint from hunger in the streets (2).
summary: Acrostic dirges over fallen Jerusalem: the weeping widow city (1) and the Lord's fierce anger against Zion (2).

a: The turning point and final pleas of the book. At the center the suffering man recounts his affliction yet reaches the hope that the Lord's steadfast love never ceases and his mercies are new every morning (3). The fourth poem contrasts Zion's former glory with her ruin under siege, recalling the horrors of famine (4). The closing chapter, not an acrostic, is a communal prayer asking the Lord to remember his people and restore them, ending with the trembling plea "restore us to yourself" (5).
summary: The book's hinge of hope in God's new mercies (3), Zion's ruined glory (4), and the closing prayer to be restored (5).

a: Ezekiel's inaugural vision and commissioning by the Chebar canal among the exiles. He sees the storm cloud, the four living creatures with wheels full of eyes, and above them the likeness of the glory of the Lord on a throne (1). The Lord commissions him as a prophet to a rebellious house and gives him a scroll of lamentation to eat, which tastes sweet as honey (2–3). He is made a watchman for the house of Israel, accountable for warning the wicked.
summary: Ezekiel's call by the Chebar: the chariot-throne vision of God's glory (1), eating the scroll (3), and his charge as watchman.

a: Sign-acts and oracles announcing the certain judgment of Jerusalem before its fall. Ezekiel dramatizes the siege with a brick, lies on his side, and shaves his hair (4–5); he sees the abominations in the temple and the glory of the Lord departing from it (8–11). He performs the exile's baggage sign (12), denounces false prophets and the rebellious house, and insists each person dies for his own sin, not the fathers' (18). At his wife's death he is forbidden to mourn as a sign that the temple, Israel's delight, will fall (24).
summary: Sign-acts and oracles of Jerusalem's doom: the siege brick (4), the glory departing the temple (10–11), personal responsibility (18), and the death of Ezekiel's wife (24).

a: Oracles against the surrounding nations who rejoiced over Judah's fall. Judgment falls on Ammon, Moab, Edom, and Philistia (25), then at length on Tyre, whose proud commercial empire is pictured as a splendid ship that sinks and whose ruler is depicted with the imagery of a fallen guardian cherub (26–28). The longest oracles target Egypt and Pharaoh, the great river-monster who will be drawn out and brought down to the pit among the slain (29–32).
summary: Oracles against the nations: Ammon, Moab, Edom, Philistia, the fall of proud Tyre (26–28), and judgment on Egypt and Pharaoh (29–32).

a: The turn from judgment to restoration after Jerusalem's fall. Ezekiel is reappointed watchman and word arrives that the city has fallen (33). The Lord condemns Israel's false shepherds and promises to shepherd his flock himself through one shepherd, his servant David (34). In the valley of dry bones, breath enters the slain and a vast army stands, picturing Israel's national resurrection (37), followed by the joining of two sticks into one nation and the defeat of Gog of Magog (38–39).
summary: Restoration after the fall: the watchman renewed (33), the true Shepherd (34), the valley of dry bones (37), and Gog of Magog (38–39).

a: The climactic vision of the new temple and restored land. A heavenly guide measures a vast ideal temple in precise detail (40–43), and the glory of the Lord returns to fill it, reversing its earlier departure (43). Regulations are given for the priesthood, the prince, and the sacred offerings (44–46). A river flows from beneath the temple threshold, growing ever deeper and bringing life and healing wherever it goes (47), and the land is allotted among the tribes around the city renamed "The Lord Is There" (48).
summary: The new-temple vision: the measured temple (40–42), the glory returning (43), the life-giving river (47), and the city named The Lord Is There (48).

a: The court narratives of faithfulness under pagan rule. Daniel and his friends refuse the king's food (1); Daniel interprets Nebuchadnezzar's dream of the statue of four kingdoms (2); the three friends survive the fiery furnace (3); Nebuchadnezzar is humbled to live as a beast and restored (4); Belshazzar sees the writing on the wall and Babylon falls (5); and Daniel is preserved in the lions' den under Darius (6).
summary: Court tales of faithfulness: the king's food (1), the statue dream (2), the fiery furnace (3), Nebuchadnezzar humbled (4), the writing on the wall (5), and the lions' den (6).

a: The first of Daniel's own apocalyptic visions of successive kingdoms and the end. Daniel sees four beasts rising from the sea, a little horn, and one like a son of man coming with the clouds to receive an everlasting kingdom from the Ancient of Days (7). The vision of the ram and the goat depicts Persia and Greece and a coming arrogant king (8). Reflecting on Jeremiah's seventy years, Daniel prays a great confession, and Gabriel reveals the prophecy of the seventy weeks decreed for Israel and the coming of an anointed one (9).
summary: Daniel's visions: the four beasts and the son of man (7), the ram and goat (8), and the prophecy of the seventy weeks (9).

a: Daniel's final vision, his longest and most detailed. After mourning and fasting, Daniel is strengthened by a glorious heavenly messenger who speaks of unseen spiritual conflict involving the princes of Persia and Greece (10). A detailed prophecy traces the wars of the kings of the north and south and a contemptible ruler who desecrates the sanctuary (11). The vision closes with the promise of Michael's deliverance, a resurrection to everlasting life or contempt, and the wise shining like the stars (12).
summary: Daniel's final vision: the heavenly messenger (10), the wars of north and south (11), and the resurrection with the wise shining like stars (12).
